title: Tutorial: Planning a Geography Field Trip

turns.speaker: Dr Okonkwo
turns.text: Right, so this is our planning meeting for the river fieldwork at Ashcombe. You're investigating how the river changes from source to mouth. Beth, where were you thinking of taking measurements?

turns.speaker: Beth
turns.text: We'd picked three sites. The upper site up on the moor, a middle site by the village bridge, and a lower site down near the estuary.

turns.speaker: Callum
turns.text: Although we did wonder whether three was enough. I thought maybe five sites would give a smoother picture.

turns.speaker: Dr Okonkwo
turns.text: In principle more is better, Callum, but be realistic — you have one day, and each site takes over an hour to survey properly. Rushing five sites would give you sloppy data. I'd stick with three done well.

turns.speaker: Callum
turns.text: Fair point. Three it is, then, but measured carefully.

turns.speaker: Dr Okonkwo
turns.text: Good. Now, the key thing in fieldwork is safety, and rivers are genuinely dangerous. What's your plan for the estuary site in particular?

turns.speaker: Beth
turns.text: That's the one that worries me, because of the tide. My idea was to check the tide times in advance and only work there at low tide.

turns.speaker: Dr Okonkwo
turns.text: Absolutely essential. The tide there comes in remarkably fast. Check the tide tables the night before, and never let anyone wade in above the knee. Wellingtons, not waders. Now let's talk about what you'll actually measure and who does what. There are three main variables: the width of the channel, the depth, and the speed of the flow.

turns.speaker: Callum
turns.text: I'm happy to handle the flow speed. I've used the flow meters before in the lab, so I know how they work.

turns.speaker: Beth
turns.text: Then I'll do the depth readings — I don't mind getting my boots wet, and I'm careful about taking readings at regular intervals across the channel.

turns.speaker: Dr Okonkwo
turns.text: And that leaves the channel width. Who's your third member — Priya, isn't it? She's not here today. Put her on width; it's the most straightforward, using the tape measure, so she can pick it up quickly when she joins you. I'll oversee the whole thing and keep an eye on safety.

turns.speaker: Beth
turns.text: Perfect. What about recording the results — on paper, or digitally?

turns.speaker: Dr Okonkwo
turns.text: Good question. I know the temptation is to type straight into a phone, but out in the field, in the rain, phones fail. Use waterproof paper and a pencil — pencil still writes when it's wet, ink doesn't. You can type it all up back at base that evening.

turns.speaker: Callum
turns.text: That makes sense. And how should we present the final findings? A written report?

turns.speaker: Dr Okonkwo
turns.text: For this module, not a report — I'd like a poster. It forces you to be selective and to make your graphs do the talking. Keep the text short and let the data speak. Aim to show clearly how each variable changes downstream.

turns.speaker: Beth
turns.text: A poster — got it. One last thing: the weather forecast for Thursday looks poor. Should we move the trip?

turns.speaker: Dr Okonkwo
turns.text: Let's not cancel for a bit of drizzle — fieldwork happens in real weather. But if there's heavy rain, the river rises fast and becomes unsafe, so I'll make the final call on Wednesday evening and email you both. Bring waterproofs regardless.

turns.speaker: Callum
turns.text: Understood. Thanks — I feel much clearer about it all now.
